name: Karri Saarinen's Quality/Craft Doctrine (Linear)
definition_origin: Karri Saarinen — Finnish designer, formerly Principal Designer at Airbnb (creator of the Airbnb Cereal typeface and DLS — Design Language System) and Head of Design at Coinbase, co-founder and CEO of Linear (founded 2019). The doctrine was articulated through his Figma Config 2025 keynote 'Crafting Quality That Endures' (May 2025), the Figma Blog post 'Karri Saarinen's 10 Rules for Crafting Products That Stand Out' (May 2025), the First Round Review podcast 'Inside Linear: Why craft and focus still win in product building' (2024), Lenny's Newsletter 'Inside Linear: Building with taste, craft, and focus' (2024), and his personal site karrisaarinen.com (essays including 'Your Product Needs a Soul' and 'On Taste, Jiro's Dream').
core_mechanism: The doctrine treats quality as a competitive moat in a market where AI codegen and SaaS templates have collapsed the cost of building 'good enough' products. Where commodity software competes on features and price, craft-led products compete on a hard-to-replicate gestalt of taste, restraint, opinionatedness, and patience. Saarinen's mechanism: small high-trust teams + opinionated direction + patience + intuition over data + treating quality as the leading metric → produces software that triggers a felt-quality signal users describe as 'this just feels different,' which compounds into organic word-of-mouth growth. The opposite is the optimization-driven, A/B-tested, feature-bloated product where every screen is a local maximum but the whole feels generic.
neuroscience_correlate: Saarinen does not invoke neuroscience explicitly, but the doctrine maps to the aesthetic-usability heuristic (Kurosu & Kashimura 1995) and to processing-fluency theory — products that feel 'crafted' produce a fluency signal interpreted as trust. [uncertain]
empirical_evidence: Linear is the doctrine's living evidence: from 0 to 10,000+ paying customers and >$50M ARR (per Lenny's Newsletter 2024) without paid marketing, almost entirely on organic referral driven by craft. Saarinen reports Linear maintains a deliberately small product team (under 60 engineers/designers as of 2024) yet ships a competitive issue-tracker against Atlassian's Jira (workforce >10,000). Adjacent evidence: Airbnb's Cereal typeface project (which Saarinen led 2017-2018) is documented in his Medium post 'Working Type' as proof that craft-driven typography produced measurable brand differentiation and reduced platform inconsistency across 60+ countries. The doctrine is empirically softer than RCT-style claims — its strongest validation is replication: Linear's playbook has been adopted at Vercel (Rauno Freiberg), Cron (Raphael Schaad, acquired by Notion), Raycast, Phantom, and others.
measurable_outcome_metric: Organic referral rate, NPS, retention (D30/D90), inbound inquiry quality, qualitative 'how did you hear about us' attribution to peer recommendation, share of design-press features (Mobbin, Page Flows, Refactoring UI). Saarinen explicitly rejects A/B-test conversion lift as the primary metric — 'You can't expect data to tell you how to surprise users.'
engineering_tactics: Implementation patterns drawn from the 10 Rules + podcast appearances. (1) LEADERSHIP COMMITMENT — quality must be the CEO's #1 explicit priority, not a poster on a wall. Saarinen: 'You have to set the tone that craft is the most important priority.' (2) SMALL TEAMS — 'Small, high-quality teams produce good work, quickly.' Linear keeps product squads at 3-5 people. (3) NO HANDOFFS — 'Everyone is responsible for the quality. There's no handoff to dev.' Designers code, engineers design, the whole product team owns the experience. (4) NO SPECIALIZATION SILOS — rotate ownership; 'Users don't care about your org chart — they care about the experience.' (5) SPEC AS BASELINE — 'For quality, you need a team that views the spec as the baseline, not the finish line.' Finishing the ticket is not finishing the work; the work ends when the experience feels right. (6) QUALITY BEFORE PERFECTION — beta is acceptable, but a clear quality bar must be hit before customer exposure. (7) OPINIONATED DESIGN — 'You should design something for someone, and it's really hard to design something really good for everyone.' Pick a target user and decline features that would dilute their experience; 'I don't believe that you can build the optimal tool for anything if it's very flexible and endlessly customizable.' (8) REDUCE SCOPE — 'Quality isn't binary — it's about continuously refining a product to meet a standard.' Cut features rather than ship rushed ones. (9) VALUES OVER PROCESS — 'Establish values over rigid processes' so teams can self-direct. (10) INTUITION OVER DATA — 'To provide the best experience, you must surprise users. You can't expect data to tell you how.' (11) DESIGN AS SEARCH — 'Design is search, not a production pipeline.' Early design is exploratory; tools that compress the search space (Figma over-systemization, AI codegen rushing to fidelity) destroy the search. (12) PATIENCE AND FOCUS — 'The more you can focus your efforts into one direction, the better outcome you probably will have. Speed is not always the most important thing and maybe scale is not always the most important thing.' Practical implementation: install a quality review ritual (Saarinen does a Friday review of every shipped surface), maintain a written design doctrine doc, refuse to A/B test feel-quality decisions, hire for taste signals (portfolio depth) over output volume.
real_world_examples: (1) LINEAR — every screen exemplifies the doctrine: 175ms motion, Inter Display headings, indigo #5E6AD2, CMD+K command palette, Linear Method written-doctrine document (linear.app/method). (2) AIRBNB CEREAL TYPEFACE (2017-18) — Saarinen led the project not because Airbnb 'needed a font' but because the absence of a brand voice produced visual inconsistency across 60+ countries; the Cereal project is documented in 'Working Type' as a craft investment with no immediate ROI but compounding brand equity. (3) LINEAR'S 'NOW' MARKETING PAGES — linear.app/now articles (e.g., 'How we redesigned the Linear UI', 'Design for the AI age') are themselves craft artifacts: WebGL animations, considered photography, written by the team. (4) RAUNO FREIBERG (Vercel) — adopted parallel doctrine; his blog posts on craft (rauno.me) cite Saarinen and emphasize the same patience-over-speed principle. (5) THINGS 3 / CULTURED CODE — pre-existing example of the doctrine; remains independent and unfunded for >15 years to preserve craft autonomy. (6) NOTION — partial adoption (Ivan Zhao's design fixation), though scaled team has produced some drift. (7) PHANTOM WALLET 2024 REBRAND — explicitly cited Saarinen's posts as inspiration for typeface investment.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: The doctrine is platform-agnostic — applies equally to mobile and web. Saarinen has acknowledged that mobile constrains opinionatedness because platform conventions (iOS/Material) are stronger gravity wells; harder to be Linear-distinctive on mobile than on web. He still recommends opinionated divergence at the brand layer (custom motion curves, custom typography, custom copy register) even when adopting platform navigation patterns.
platform_design_language_alignment: Doctrine is META to platform languages — opposes treating Material 3 Expressive or Liquid Glass as ceilings. Saarinen would say: adopt the platform default as a baseline (Rule 5: 'spec as baseline'), then override the layers that carry your brand. Linear adopts iOS/Material navigation patterns but overrides typography, color, motion, and copy register. Material 3 Expressive's enthusiasm for 'expressiveness' aligns with the opinionated-design rule. Liquid Glass's translucency is fine if it's an opinionated brand choice, dangerous if adopted defensively because Apple suggested it.
claude_design_application: Use the doctrine as a constraint stack on Claude prompts — it forbids generic outputs. Prompt fragment 1: 'Build this component following Karri Saarinen's craft doctrine. Specifically: (a) treat the spec as a baseline, push beyond — name two refinements you would add beyond the spec, (b) design opinionated for a specific user (specify: senior software engineers using keyboard-first), (c) motion: exact 175ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1), no exceptions, (d) typography: Inter Display for headings, Inter for body, no other fonts, (e) restrict to 3 corner-radius tokens (4px, 6px, 8px), (f) write copy in declarative third-person, no exclamation marks. Output the token JSON FIRST so I can verify discipline before reading the JSX.' Prompt fragment 2: 'Refactor this component for craft. Apply Saarinen's principles: (1) reduce scope — what can be removed without harming the core flow? (2) eliminate handoff seams — does the empty state, error state, and loading state share the same visual language as the success state? (3) opinionated copy — replace any "Click here" with a specific verb. (4) motion review — name the exact easing and duration of every transition; if any is the framework default, replace with a brand-tokenized curve.'
stitch_application: Stitch is well-suited to articulating the doctrine as a constraint set. Prompt fragment 1: 'Mockup an empty state for a project-tracker following Karri Saarinen\'s craft doctrine. Surface: deep ink #1E1E22. Headline 28px Inter Display Medium, color #FAFAFA. Body 14px Inter Regular, color #9CA3AF. Single accent — Linear indigo #5E6AD2 — used only on the CTA button (32px height, 6px radius). No illustration. Copy must be opinionated: "Nothing here yet. Press C to create your first issue." Note the keyboard shortcut hint embedded in copy. No emoji, no exclamation marks, no third-person help text.' Prompt fragment 2: 'Mockup a settings page with Saarinen\'s reduce-scope principle applied. Show only 5 settings groups maximum, each with 3 settings maximum. Use 8px grid. Section headers: 12px Inter Medium, uppercase, letter-spacing 0.5px, color #6B6B6B. Setting labels: 14px Inter Regular, color #FAFAFA. Right-aligned current value or toggle. Single divider line #2A2A2A between groups. No icons next to settings unless they aid recognition. Forbid: card backgrounds, drop shadows, gradients, more than 5 groups.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen butchers the doctrine in three predictable ways. (1) Speed over patience — AI rushes from spec to JSX, skipping the search phase Saarinen calls essential; 'design is search, not a production pipeline' is exactly what AI codegen flattens. Counter: prompt for 3 distinct directional sketches BEFORE the implementation, with reasoning why one is chosen. (2) Default-to-generic — AI outputs Inter on white with blue button when given any spec, ignoring opinionated brand tokens. Counter: include the token doctrine in every prompt and verify the JSON tokens are echoed in the output before reviewing JSX. (3) Optimization-first — AI suggests A/B-testable variants, microcopy 'optimization,' or feature additions when the doctrine demands restraint. Counter: instruct 'reduce scope, do not add features, do not propose A/B variants — propose only refinements that increase felt quality.' Saarinen on AI specifically (Lenny's Newsletter 2024): AI is a useful 'fast hands' but cannot substitute for the patience phase; teams that out-source taste to AI will produce indistinguishable products.
anti_patterns: (1) Quality-as-poster — claiming craft is a value while shipping ticket-by-ticket without a quality bar. (2) Hiring 'output volume' designers/engineers instead of taste-and-judgment people. (3) Letting handoff seams form — designer hands Figma to engineer, engineer ships 'close enough' pixel-shifts, no quality review. (4) Over-customizing for every customer request — the 'we'll add a setting for that' trap dilutes opinion until the product is amorphous. (5) A/B-testing brand-feel decisions — testing button copy is fine; testing whether to keep custom typography is doctrine violation. (6) Confusing patience with slowness — Saarinen ships fast within the search phase, then slow on the polish phase. The doctrine is not 'go slow always.' (7) Mistaking 'opinionated' for 'rude' — opinionated design serves the chosen user better, it does not exclude empathy.
regulatory_risk: Low directly. Indirectly, opinionated design that excludes accessibility-first considerations (small font sizes, low-contrast 'aesthetic' choices, motion-heavy interactions without prefers-reduced-motion) creates EAA (June 2025 enforcement) and ADA Title III exposure. Saarinen's doctrine does not explicitly address accessibility — implementation teams must layer WCAG 2.2 AA compliance on top.
pitfalls_caveats: (1) The doctrine assumes a passionate small team — does not scale linearly past 100-150 people without significant cultural reinforcement, and Saarinen has acknowledged this is the next challenge for Linear. (2) Survivorship bias — Linear is the loud success; many craft-first startups fail because their TAM was too small or they were out-executed by faster generalist competitors. (3) Hard to teach explicitly — taste is calibrated by years of exposure to good work; junior teams adopting the doctrine without senior craft leaders produce craft-cargo-cult outputs (the visible artifacts of distinctiveness without the underlying judgment). (4) Localization tension — opinionated design that targets 'senior software engineers in SF' may underperform in JP/KR markets where formality conventions differ. (5) Performance — heavy motion and custom typefaces have a frame-budget cost on low-end Android.
cross_cultural_validity: The doctrine itself (small teams, opinionated direction, patience, taste) translates globally — Japanese craft traditions (the 'Jiro's Dream' essay on Saarinen's site explicitly references Sukiyabashi Jiro) provide the strongest cultural prior. Specific TOKENS Linear uses (English-first copy register, Inter for CJK fallback, dark theme defaulting to engineer-aesthetic) require localization. Saarinen has cited Apple, Sony, and Muji as cross-cultural exemplars of the doctrine; suggests it sits naturally in cultures with strong craft traditions (JP, Nordics, parts of CN) and translates with effort to growth-optimization-dominant cultures (US Silicon Valley default, India's pragmatic engineering culture).
sources: [Karri Saarinen — 10 Rules for Crafting Products That Stand Out (Figma Blog)](https://www.figma.com/blog/karri-saarinens-10-rules-for-crafting-products-that-stand-out/)
